---
title: "Building the alldata species sensitivity dataset"
subtitle: "Pipeline methods, decisions, and deviations from the ANZG derivation procedure"
author: "ssddata authors"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output: rmarkdown::html_vignette
bibliography: "../inst/REFERENCES.bib"
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{Building the alldata species sensitivity dataset}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---

```{r setup, include = FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  collapse = TRUE,
  comment = "#>"
)
```

## Overview

This vignette describes the processing applied across all the available datasets to construct a single "all" chronic dataset following the @Warne2025ANZG protocol as much as is possible via reproducible computation, supplemented in some cases via manual curation and LLM via Claude Sonnet and Opus models provided by Anthropic and OpenAI, respectively. The resulting dataset is the `all_chronic` set returned by `ssd_data_sets(set = "all_chronic")`.

`ssd_data_sets(set = "all_chronic")` returns a named list of species-sensitivity
datasets, one element per chemical × medium combination (e.g. `copper_marine`),
each giving one toxicity value per species. Where a chemical has too few data in
any single medium, a pooled `mixed` set is provided instead. This vignette
describes how those sets are constructed.

The aggregation procedure follows the method of @Warne2025ANZG, the
authoritative technical reference for deriving Australian and New Zealand water
quality guideline values (ANZG guidelines). Key sections of that document are
cited throughout. Where this pipeline deviates from the @Warne2025ANZG
procedure — whether because of data limitations, because it produces a dataset
for methodology testing rather than official guideline derivation, or because of
deliberate conservative choices — the deviations are documented explicitly in
the [Deviations appendix](#sec-deviations).

---

## Data sources {#sec-sources}

The `all_chronic` dataset integrates seven sources, divided into curated and uncurated
categories.

**Curated sources** have been prepared and quality-assured by the organisations
that hold them. They are used as-is, subject only to schema harmonisation and
deduplication:

- **anzg** — Australian and New Zealand Guidelines dataset [@Warne2025ANZG]; highest priority in
  the deduplication hierarchy.
- **ccme** — Canadian Council of Ministers of the Environment dataset.
- **aims** — Australian Institute of Marine Science dataset.
- **csiro** — Commonwealth Scientific and Industrial Research Organisation dataset.

**Uncurated sources** are large, heterogeneous databases. They require more
extensive pre-processing before they can be aggregated:

- **anztox** — a PostgreSQL database of ecotoxicological data maintained
  internally [@Sunderam2000ANZECCdb; @ANZECC2000Guidelines].
- **wqbench** — an R package providing access to the ECOTOX database
  [@epa2025ecotox] (Chapman et al.); intercepted before the package's own
  aggregation step runs, so that raw records are available for deduplication
  against other sources.
- **envirotox** — the EnviroTox database [@Connors2019], provided as an Excel workbook.

The `anon_` datasets distributed with the package are excluded from `alldata`
because they lack chemical identifiers.

---

## Stage 1 — Schema audit {#sec-stage1}

Before any data were extracted, a schema audit documented the column names,
data types, and value vocabularies in each of the three uncurated sources. The
audit identified that the fields required by @Warne2025ANZG Section
3.4.4 aggregation procedure — specifically `statistic_type`, `duration_hours`,
`effect_category`, and `life_stage` — were available in varying forms across the
three sources and would require harmonisation.

The audit output is `data-raw/alldata/scripts/stage1-schema-audit.md`.

---

## Stage 2 — CAS number and chemical name alignment {#sec-stage2}

Different sources use different CAS numbers for the same chemical (e.g.
speciation variants of metals, salt forms of organic compounds). A parent-CAS
lookup table maps source-specific CAS numbers to a canonical parent CAS number
and chemical name for each chemical group.

The lookup was built in five sub-stages (2a–2e), combining:

- A 40-row human-curated seed lookup for known speciation cases.
- Heuristic rules that identify CAS numbers sharing a parent by name pattern.
- LLM-assisted enrichment of ambiguous cases.
- Manual review of uncertain assignments.

The master lookup is `data-raw/cas_parent_lookup_all.csv` (587 rows). Eighteen
rows remain flagged UNCERTAIN pending domain expert review; these affect Stage 6
deduplication and are documented in the deferred decisions log
(`data-raw/alldata/scripts/stage3-deferred-decisions.md`).

Speciation decisions and rules are documented in
`data-raw/anztox/speciation_convention.md` and
`scripts/speciation_policy_extensions.md`.

The build process for this lookup is documented in full in
`vignettes/cas_parent_lookup_build.qmd`.

---

## Stage 3 — Media assignment {#sec-stage3}

Each record is assigned to a medium: Freshwater, Marine, or Unknown. The ANZG
guideline values are derived separately for freshwater and marine environments,
so medium assignment is a prerequisite for all subsequent grouping and filtering.

- **anztox**: medium is available as a controlled field; values are normalised
  to the three-value vocabulary.
- **wqbench**: medium was added via PR #43 (`add_media_wqbench`).
- **envirotox**: medium is not reliably available; all envirotox records are
  assigned "Unknown".
- **ccme**: all records carry `medium == "Freshwater"` as observed in the source
  data, pending full confirmation against the original source (Issue #34).

The ANZG medium field uses five variants (hard/moderate/soft freshwater, marine,
estuarine freshwater). These are not collapsed — the distinctions are preserved
for downstream use.

---

## Stage 4 — Uncurated source processing {#sec-stage4} 
### a — Pipeline audit (uncurated sources) {#sec-stage4a}

A targeted audit of each uncurated source's extraction pipeline established the
following:

- **anztox**: concentration units for the `concentrationused` field are µg/L for
  the 2000 dataset subset (86% of records, confirmed via supplementary DB query)
  and assumed µg/L for the 2016 dataset subset (14%), with uncertainty flagged
  via a `source_dataset` column.
- **wqbench**: must be intercepted before `wqb_aggregate()` runs so that raw
  records are available for deduplication; the package's own aggregation is not
  used.
- **envirotox**: the DATASET.R solubility and type filter is reproduced at
  interception to ensure the same record set.
- 632 "Chronic QSAR" rows in anztox were deliberately excluded (modelled values,
  not experimental data).

The full audit is `data-raw/alldata/scripts/stage4a-pipeline-audit.md`.

---

### b — Extract filtered unaggregated records {#sec-stage4b}

Raw unaggregated records were extracted from all three uncurated sources into a
common 17-column schema (`uncurated_raw_combined.csv`). The schema includes all
fields required by the @Warne2025ANZG Section 3.4.4 aggregation: `effect_category`,
`statistic_type`, `duration_hours`, and `life_stage`, as well as the `acr_eligible`
flag (TRUE where `statistic_type` ∈ {EC50, LC50, IC50}).

Source record counts after extraction: anztox 15,667 / wqbench 361,782 /
envirotox 72,439. Total: 449,888 rows.

The extraction script is `data-raw/alldata/scripts/stage4b-extract.R` but requires access to the original anztox SQL database.

---

### c — Cross-source deduplication {#sec-stage4c}

#### Why deduplication before priority selection?

A key architectural decision (Decision I2) is that cross-source deduplication
is applied *before* ANZG priority selection (chronic > subchronic > acute within
a source). The alternative — applying priority selection first, then deduplicating
— risks discarding chronic data from a lower-priority source in favour of acute
data from a higher-priority source. The chosen order ensures that a chronic
record from, say, envirotox is not lost because an acute record for the same
observation exists in a higher-priority source.

#### Deduplication procedure

Cross-source duplicates were identified using a six-field key:

`native_cas × scientificname_norm × medium × statistic_type_norm × effect_category × duration_hours × conc_ug_L`

where `conc_ug_L` was matched with a 0.1% tolerance to accommodate floating-point
representation differences. `NA` values in key fields were treated strictly — a
row with NA duration does not match a row with a non-NA duration.

For true cross-source duplicates, one record is retained per the priority order:
`wqbench > anztox > envirotox` (for the uncurated sources). All records are
flagged rather than dropped, so the full record of what was found is preserved.

Within-source duplicates were detected and flagged separately (anztox 9.2%, wqbench 25.1%, envirotox 0.56%). The elevated wqbench rate warrants explanation. The wqbench package constructs its prepared dataset from raw US EPA ECOTOX data using wqb_create_data_set(), which discards the ECOTOX test_id and result_id fields in the process. Without these provenance identifiers, records can only be distinguished by their data content. In a comprehensive database like ECOTOX, it is common for genuinely distinct experiments — conducted by different laboratories or under different test conditions — to produce records that share the same species, chemical, concentration, toxicity class, and endpoint values. The within-source duplicate check used in this pipeline could not distinguish those cases from true data entry duplicates. Resolving this would require bypassing wqb_create_data_set() and working directly from raw ECOTOX data, at which point the source would no longer be wqbench; that approach is out of scope for this pipeline. The flagged rows were retained and handled correctly by the Section 3.4.4 geometric mean aggregation step, which produces an identical result whether collapsing true duplicates or coincident distinct records.

After deduplication and priority selection, the final clean subset contains
381,410 rows. The audit report is `data-raw/alldata/stage4c-dedup-report.md`.

#### Effect category harmonisation

The three uncurated sources (anztox, wqbench, envirotox) record the *type of
biological effect measured* using mutually incompatible vocabularies. These were
mapped onto a single controlled vocabulary of 13 codes, applied per-source through
keyword rules and lookup tables (`envirotox_effect_category_map.csv`,
`anztox_2016_effect_category_map.csv`) via `data-raw/alldata/scripts/stage4c-effect-category-fixup.R`,
with a post-fix assertion guaranteeing every non-NA value is one of the 13. Effect
category is orthogonal to the statistic type (NOEC, EC10, LC50): the effect
category records *what was measured*, the statistic type records *how the threshold
was estimated*. The curated sources (anzg, ccme, aims, csiro) carry no effect
category and take `NA`.

The 13 codes, with their meaning and their tier under @Warne2025ANZG Section 3.2.1:

| Code | Meaning | Warne §3.2.1 tier |
|------|---------|-------------------|
| `MORT` | Mortality | Traditional |
| `IMM`  | Immobilisation | Traditional |
| `GRO`  | Growth | Traditional |
| `DVP`  | Development | Traditional |
| `POP`  | Population | Traditional |
| `REP`  | Reproduction | Traditional |
| `HAT`  | Hatching / hatchability | Traditional |
| `ABD`  | Abundance | Traditional |
| `PSE`  | Photosynthesis | Non-traditional |
| `BCH`  | Biochemistry / physiology | Non-traditional |
| `BEH`  | Behaviour | Non-traditional |
| `LUM`  | Bioluminescence | Non-traditional |
| `MOR`  | Morphology | Non-traditional |

Source terms map in by meaning: e.g. `Mortality` / `Mortality, Growth` → `MORT`;
`Growth` / `Biomass` / `Length` → `GRO`; `Immobilization` / `Mobility` → `IMM`;
`Photosynthesis` / `Chlorophyll` / `Physiology` → `PSE`;
`Biochemistry` / `Enzyme(s)` / `Hormone(s)` → `BCH`. Terms with no ecotoxicologically
meaningful mapping (e.g. `Intoxication`, `Multiple`, `General`, `Accumulation`)
resolve to `NA` and are dropped at the NA-filter step in Stage 4e. Two
source-specific points are worth noting: anztox `ABD` historically encoded
*avoidance/behaviour* rather than abundance, and is recoded to `BEH` against the
source endpoint dictionary so that `ABD` denotes abundance only; and envirotox
`Reproduction, Hatch` resolves to `REP` (the "reproduc" keyword fires before
"hatch"), so `HAT` is populated almost entirely from explicit hatchability terms.

Approximately 23,567 records in the final clean subset have unmappable
`effect_category` values and take `NA`; these are dropped in Stage 4e.

@Warne2025ANZG Section 3.2.1 treats organism-level (apical) endpoints — survival,
immobilisation, growth, development, population growth, reproduction — as
ecologically relevant and usable directly. Non-traditional endpoints (it names
photosynthesis inhibition, in-vivo biochemical/physiological endpoints, and
behavioural endpoints) may be used only where ecological relevance has been
unequivocally demonstrated; otherwise they belong in weight-of-evidence assessment,
not the SSD dataset. Accordingly, the five non-traditional codes are excluded before
aggregation in Stage 4e, flagged with an explicit exclusion reason rather than
silently dropped. This removed 54,709 records (dominated by wqbench biochemistry),
causing 1,628 species × chemical × medium groups — 428 distinct species — to be
dropped where they retained no traditional endpoint. After the filter, every
surviving value is traditional.

Because the pipeline reduces each species × chemical × medium group to its single
lowest value, the effect category of the *selected* value is retained in the final
object as `EffectCategory`, so the endpoint underlying each datum is visible and
auditable downstream. Where several endpoints tie on the minimum concentration
(1,802 groups), the tie is broken by alphabetical order of effect category. Curated
rows carry `EffectCategory = NA` (770 rows).

The effect-category composition of the final `allchronic_data` object (uncurated
rows only; all values traditional):

| EffectCategory | Rows |
|----------------|------|
| `MORT` | 17,349 |
| `POP`  |  4,618 |
| `GRO`  |  2,076 |
| `REP`  |    751 |
| `DVP`  |    749 |
| `IMM`  |    209 |
| `ABD`  |      6 |
| `HAT`  |      5 |
| `NA` (curated) | 770 |

---

### d — Species name resolution and taxonomy enrichment {#sec-stage4d}

#### Why resolve species names before aggregation?

The @Warne2025ANZG Section 3.4.4 geometric mean is computed per species. If a species appears
under different names in different sources (e.g. an accepted name in one source
and a synonym in another), those records will be treated as different species
unless synonyms are unified first. Stage 4d resolves this before aggregation.

This ordering reversal (species resolution before aggregation, not after) was
a deliberate design decision made during Stage 4d planning.

#### Resolution procedure

Species names were resolved against the World Register of Marine Species (WoRMS)
using a context-aware querying approach: source-native taxonomy (extracted in
Stage 4d Part 1.5 from each database's own taxonomy tables) was used to
disambiguate species that WoRMS returns multiple candidate matches for. Where
WoRMS could not resolve a species, the Global Biodiversity Information Facility
(GBIF) was used as a fallback, subject to a genus-or-finer rank floor.

Of 4,348 unique species names, 99.22% were resolved to a usable taxonomic level
(4,200 species). The remaining 148 were handled via a source-native fallback
(Stage 4d Part 2 fixup) or manual correction; 12 species remained unresolvable
(placeholder labels and unresolvable misspellings, all from anztox) and were
hard-excluded (28 rows removed).

#### Synonym unification

WoRMS resolution identified 254 synonym groups. The `accepted_name` column
in the enriched dataset carries the WoRMS-accepted name for all species in
a synonym group, replacing the raw source name. This affects 46,826 rows.
The `accepted_name` column is the authoritative species identifier for Stage 4e
aggregation; the original raw name is preserved in `original_scientificname`
for audit purposes.

#### Majorgroup derivation

The ANZG eligibility check requires species counts by taxonomic group. The
`majorgroup` column is derived directly from the resolved taxonomic class
(`class`), without reference to ANZG Table 5. This is a deliberate deviation
from strict ANZG procedure — see [Deviations appendix](#sec-deviations).

The enriched dataset (`uncurated_raw_dedup_enriched.csv`) contains 115 distinct
majorgroup values, with Teleostei, Branchiopoda, Malacostraca, Insecta, and
Chlorophyceae as the most represented groups.

The audit report is `data-raw/alldata/stage4d-part3-enrichment-report.md`.

---

### e — Aggregation: one toxicity value per species {#sec-stage4e}

The aggregation procedure follows @Warne2025ANZG Section 3.4.4. The goal
is to reduce the many raw toxicity records for each species to a single
representative toxicity value per species per chemical per medium.

#### Pre-aggregation preparation

Before aggregation, three preparatory steps are applied:

1. **Unit conversion**: wqbench records are in mg/L; all other sources are in
   µg/L. wqbench `conc_value` is multiplied by 1,000 before aggregation.

2. **Coerce artefact values**: the literal string "Not stated" in the
   `majorgroup`/`class` column (4 rows carrying a source-native placeholder)
   is coerced to NA.

3. **Drop records that cannot be aggregated**:
   - Records with NA `effect_category` (~23,567 rows) are dropped because
     `effect_category` is part of the grouping key.
   - Records with a **non-traditional** `effect_category` (PSE, BCH, BEH, LUM,
     MOR) are excluded (54,709 rows); see [Stage 4c](#sec-stage4c)
     *Effect category harmonisation*.
   - Records where `test_class == "acute"` and `acr_eligible != TRUE` are
     dropped. These are acute NOECs, LOECs, and similar values that cannot
     be converted to chronic equivalents per @Warne2025ANZG Section 3.4.2.2:
     *"acute EC/IC/LCx (where x ≠ 50), NOEC, LOEC and MATC values
     must not be used to derive chronic GVs"*.
   - Records whose `statistic_type` has no defined @Warne2025ANZG treatment —
     undefined ECx percentiles (20 < x < 50 or x > 50) and mammalian/regulatory
     summary endpoints (NOAEL, LOAEL, NOAEC, LOAEC) — are excluded (1,278 rows;
     see *Statistic-type hierarchy and conversions* below).
   - Uncurated records that resolve only to **genus rank** (a genus-only
     `accepted_name` — a bare genus, or a name carrying an `sp.`/`spp.`/`cf.`/
     `aff.`/`nr.` qualifier) are dropped (9,638 rows, 721 distinct names). A
     genus-level identification may represent any of several species in that
     genus, so it cannot stand in for a single species in an SSD. This reverses
     an earlier decision to retain such entries (see Appendix A, R1). Curated
     sources are unaffected — they do not pass through Stage 4e. Before this
     filter, eight raw binomials that had floored to genus because of outdated or
     misspelled names were recovered to species via manual corrections (for
     example "Salmo gardieri" → *Oncorhynchus mykiss*, "Hizikia fusiforme" →
     *Sargassum fusiforme*); the full triage record is
     `data-raw/alldata/stage4e-genus-rank-decisions.md`.

### Statistic-type hierarchy and conversions

@Warne2025ANZG Section 3.2.4 ranks the statistical estimates of toxicity "in
order of preference". Some chronic estimates are accepted as negligible-effect values and
used as-is; others are median- or low-effect estimates that must be converted
before use (Section 3.4.2.1); acute median-effect values sit lowest and are
converted via an acute-to-chronic ratio (Section 3.4.2.2). The uncurated sources
arrive with a mix of all of these — and, importantly, the wqbench records are
intercepted before that package applies its own endpoint selection, so the
hierarchy is the alldata pipeline's responsibility to apply. Stage 4e implements
it in three parts: classification, conversion, and a preference filter.

#### Classification

Each record's `statistic_type` is classified into one of three actions:

| Action | Statistic types | Treatment |
|---|---|---|
| **accepted** | NEC, NSEC, BEC10, NOEC, NOEL, and EC/IC/LCx with x ≤ 20 | used as-is |
| **convert** | EC50/IC50/LC50, LOEC/LOEL, MATC (chronic/subchronic) | divided by a conversion factor |
| **exclude** | EC/IC/LCx with 20 < x < 50 or x > 50; NOAEL, LOAEL, NOAEC, LOAEC, and unrecognised codes (MCIG, etc.) | removed |

The percentile *x* in any `ECx`/`ICx`/`LCx` code is parsed (including decimal
values such as `EC2.52`) so that low-effect percentiles are correctly recognised
as accepted estimates. Codes that @Warne2025ANZG provides no treatment for —
percentiles between the low-effect and median-effect bands, percentiles above
50%, and mammalian/regulatory summary endpoints (NOAEL, LOAEL, and similar) — are
excluded; there is no defensible way to place them on a negligible-effect SSD
axis. A total of 1,278 records were excluded on these grounds, with the full
listing written to `data-raw/alldata/stage4e-statistic-type-excluded.csv`.

#### Conversion

Two conversions are applied, both before aggregation so that all concentrations
are on a common negligible-effect, chronic-equivalent footing:

- **Chronic median- and low-effect conversion** [@Warne2025ANZG, Section 3.4.2.1].
  For chronic and subchronic records: EC50/IC50/LC50 are divided by 5, LOEC/LOEL
  by 2.5, and MATC by 2. These are recorded in `chronic_conv_factor` and flagged
  with `chronic_conv_applied`. 66,566 records were converted at this step.
- **Acute-to-chronic conversion** [@Warne2025ANZG, Section 3.4.2.2]. Acute
  EC50/IC50/LC50 records are divided by the default ACR of 10 and flagged with
  `acr_applied` (142,475 records). Acute non-50 percentiles, NOECs, LOECs, and
  MATCs cannot be converted and were already dropped.

Section 3.4.2.1 frames the chronic conversion as a conditional fallback used when
preferred negligible-effect data are insufficient, rather than an automatic
transform of every median-effect value. The preference filter below realises that
intent: a converted value is only ever used for a species when nothing better
exists for it.

#### Preference filter

@Warne2025ANZG ranks the three tiers — accepted negligible-effect estimates
first, converted chronic median/low-effect estimates second, ACR-converted acute
estimates last. The pipeline applies this ranking at the level of each
**species × chemical × medium** combination, irrespective of endpoint, duration,
or life stage: within that combination, only records of the best tier present are
retained, and all lower-tier records are dropped. A species therefore contributes
a converted or acute-derived value only when it has no accepted negligible-effect
record for that chemical and medium at all.

This filter removed 71,695 records. Of the 66,566 chronic conversions applied,
56,347 were subsequently suppressed because an accepted record existed for the
same species × chemical × medium — only 10,219 converted records survived to
aggregation, confirming that the conversion behaves as a genuine last resort
rather than a routine adjustment.

The filter also resolves a subtler conflict. In 2,823 species × chemical × medium
combinations, a species held both chronic and acute records that were not
cross-source duplicates (they came from different sources under different test
conditions). Without an explicit ranking, the lowest-wins aggregation that
follows could allow an ACR-converted acute value to displace a genuine chronic
value. The tier preference prevents this, keeping the chronic data in every such
case, consistent with @Warne2025ANZG Section 3.4.2.2 ("the use of chronic
toxicity data is always preferred").

Each output row records the tier its value came from in `value_tier`
(`accepted`, `chronic_converted`, or `acute_acr`) and carries
`any_chronic_conv_applied` as a convenience flag.

### Concentration plausibility filter

Before aggregation begins, each record is assessed for plausibility against
four concentration thresholds:

| Category | Threshold | Action |
|---|---|---|
| Hard low | < 10⁻⁵ µg/L | Excluded before aggregation |
| Soft low | 10⁻⁵ – 10⁻³ µg/L | Retained; flagged as `low_soft` |
| Normal | 10⁻³ – 10⁶ µg/L | Retained without flag |
| Soft high | 10⁶ – 10⁸ µg/L | Retained; flagged as `high_soft` |
| Hard high | > 10⁸ µg/L | Excluded before aggregation |

The lower hard threshold (10⁻⁵ µg/L = 10 pg/L) reflects the practical floor
for reliably reproduced aquatic toxicity endpoints. While modern analytical
detection limits can reach sub-pg/L concentrations, reported effect thresholds
below 1 ng/L (10⁻³ µg/L) are extremely rare and, when they appear in large
ecotoxicological databases, are almost always attributable to unit errors,
misreported nominal concentrations, or statistical extrapolation below the
tested range. The upper hard threshold (10⁸ µg/L = 100 g/L) exceeds the
solubility ceiling for essentially all compounds in aqueous systems.

Soft-flagged records are retained because concentrations in the soft range,
while unusual, are not impossible — particularly for potent endocrine disruptors
or highly soluble salts at extreme test concentrations. The soft flag propagates
through the aggregation: the geometric mean is computed using only ok-range
records; soft-flagged records contribute only where no ok-range records exist
for a group (a "soft-only fallback"). Output rows derived from such fallback
groups carry `any_conc_flagged = TRUE`.

54 records were hard-excluded, all from wqbench (three fewer than the
pre-exclusion run; those three were genus-rank rows removed earlier — see the
drop step below). The low-end cases still include a cluster of *Daphnia
magna* and *Ceriodaphnia dubia* NOEC and LOEC values at exactly 1×10⁻⁶ µg/L
for a single chemical (CAS 70288867), consistent with a systematic unit error
in the source data for that chemical. A further 3,280 records were soft-flagged
and retained. The plausibility filter resolved all physically implausible output
values present in the initial aggregation run.

#### Step 1: geometric mean within endpoint combinations

Following @Warne2025ANZG Section 3.4.4, the geometric mean is computed within each unique
combination of:

> `casnumber_grouped × accepted_name × medium × effect_category × statistic_type × duration_hours × life_stage`

This grouping key implements the @Warne2025ANZG Section 3.4.4 requirement that geometric means
are not calculated across different statistical estimates of toxicity, life
stages, or test durations.

**Handling of NA in `life_stage` and `duration_hours`**: Both fields have
records where the value is not available (NA). NA is treated as a distinct
group level — it does not merge with any named value. A record with NA
life_stage and a record with life_stage "Neonate" are in separate groups,
even if all other key fields match. This is consistent with the conservative
approach applied throughout the pipeline: unknown values are not assumed to
be equivalent to known ones. Audit flags (`lifestage_mixed`, `duration_mixed`)
are set where both NA and non-NA values from a single source contribute to
the same endpoint minimum in Step 2.

**Geometric mean spread check**: If values within a group span more than one
order of magnitude (max/min > 10), the geometric mean is considered
unreliable [@Warne2025ANZG, Section 3.4.4]. In such cases, the minimum
value is used instead, and the `geomean_flagged` column is set to TRUE. This
implements the most conservative of the two options @Warne2025ANZG suggest for
professional judgement (selecting the lowest value).

#### Step 2: lowest value within each endpoint per species

Within each `casnumber_grouped × accepted_name × medium × effect_category`
combination, the minimum is taken across all `statistic_type × duration_hours
× life_stage` combinations. This is the lowest geometric-mean value for that
endpoint.

#### Step 3: lowest value across endpoints per species

Within each `casnumber_grouped × accepted_name × medium` combination, the
minimum is taken across all `effect_category` values. This is the single
toxicity value representing the sensitivity of the species for that chemical
in that medium.

#### Output

The Stage 4e output is one row per `casnumber_grouped × accepted_name × medium`
combination. Key provenance columns are summarised:

- `n_records`: number of raw records that contributed to the aggregated value.
- `sources_contributing`: comma-separated list of source names.
- `value_tier`: the best tier present for that species × chemical × medium
  (`accepted`, `chronic_converted`, or `acute_acr`).
- `any_acr_applied`: TRUE if any contributing record had ACR conversion applied.
- `any_chronic_conv_applied`: TRUE if any contributing record had chronic
  median/low-effect conversion applied.
- `geomean_flagged`: TRUE if any geometric mean was replaced by a minimum due
  to the >1 order of magnitude spread rule.
- `any_conc_flagged`: TRUE if the aggregated value is derived exclusively from
  soft-flagged concentration records (no ok-range records existed in any
  contributing group).

The output contains 59,177 rows across 5,671 chemicals and 3,041 species. Of
these, 13,717 derive from accepted negligible-effect estimates, 4,224 from
converted chronic median/low-effect estimates, and 41,236 from ACR-converted
acute estimates. The acute share is large because the wqbench source is
composed almost entirely of acute LC/EC50 data.

The audit report is `data-raw/alldata/stage4e-aggregation-report.md`. Stage 4e is implemented in `data-raw/alldata/DATASET.R` (see [Stage 6](#sec-stage6)).

---

## Stage 5 — Units normalisation {#sec-stage5}

The wqbench mg/L → µg/L unit conversion was folded into Stage 4e (applied
before aggregation). A separate Stage 5 is not required. The heading is retained here for reproducivility purposes to ensure git commit messages, audit reports, claude prompt logs and any other references to "Stage 5" (and subsquent stage numnbering) remain consistent with the pipeline documentation.

---

## Stage 6 — Integration with curated sources and source priority {#sec-stage6}

Stage 6 combines the aggregated uncurated dataset from Stage 4e with the four
curated sources — ANZG, CCME, AIMS, and CSIRO — and resolves overlaps by a strict
source-priority hierarchy. Stages 4e, 6, and 7 are all implemented in a single
script, `data-raw/alldata/DATASET.R`: Stage 4e runs first and passes its
aggregated output directly in-memory to Stage 6, with no separate
`alldata_integrated.csv` intermediate.

### CAS mapping and curated source preparation

Curated `.rda` objects carry no CAS number, so each curated chemical is mapped to
a `casnumber_grouped` via `data-raw/alldata/curated_cas_lookup.csv` (see
`vignettes/cas_parent_lookup_build.qmd` for the master lookup build). The master
lookup is filtered to `excluded == FALSE`, removing the 18 UNCERTAIN CAS rows.

The four curated sources fall into two tiers:

- **ANZG and CCME** are jurisdictionally authoritative and used wholesale, exactly
  as supplied — no taxonomy harmonisation, no @Warne2025ANZG Section 3.4.4
  aggregation, no concentration filtering.
- **AIMS and CSIRO** are curated but not jurisdictional. Their species names are
  resolved through the same WoRMS/GBIF cache used in Stage 4d. Their *values* are
  kept as supplied, but where the same species appears more than once for a
  chemical × medium (typically the same species tested under tropical and
  temperate domains), the records are combined by geometric mean following
  @Warne2025ANZG Section 3.4.4 — the `Domain` field is not part of the grouping
  key, consistent with `.apply_dedup()` in `get_ssddata.R`. AIMS reduced from 40
  to 37 rows and CSIRO from 91 to 60 by this within-source aggregation.

Curated rows with no species name in the source are dropped rather than retained
under a placeholder taxon (decision S6-D4). This removes the 30 unnamed
chlorine/marine CSIRO records — short-term toxicity data that does not belong in a
chronic dataset — at source, rather than relying on ANZG coverage to exclude them
incidentally.

### Source priority and exclusion

Sources are ranked **ANZG > CCME > AIMS > CSIRO > uncurated**, and a chemical ×
medium combination is owned outright by its highest-priority contributor:

- If **ANZG** covers a chemical × medium, that combination *is* the ANZG dataset
  and all other sources are excluded from it. ANZG freshwater coverage is matched
  broadly — any of the freshwater variants (Freshwater, Soft / Moderate / Hard
  freshwater, nitrate variants) excludes other sources' freshwater data for that
  chemical — but the variants themselves are preserved as distinct media and never
  collapsed.
- If **CCME** covers a chemical × medium (and ANZG does not), CCME owns it.
- Otherwise **AIMS, CSIRO, and uncurated** are reconciled at the species level in
  priority order: each species appears once, taken from the highest-priority
  source that has it. AIMS and CSIRO therefore take precedence where they cover a species, while uncurated data fills in the species the curated sources lack. This is a species-level merge, in contrast to the wholesale per-chemical replacement applied for ANZG and CCME.

Exclusion counts at this stage: ANZG freshwater-family 1,192 rows, ANZG marine 947
rows, CCME 807 rows, and 29 rows from the AIMS > CSIRO > uncurated species
reconciliation. The integrated frame passed to Stage 7 contains 57,035 rows.

CCME medium values are `"Freshwater"` for all rows; confirmation against the
source is pending (Issue #34).

The audit report is `data-raw/alldata/stage6-integration-report.md`.

---

## Stage 7 — Medium viability, pooling, and the named-list data object {#sec-stage7}

Stage 7 decides which chemical × medium datasets are emitted, assembles a pooled
fallback where needed, and writes `allchronic_data` — the object behind
`ssd_data_sets(set = "all_chronic")`.

### Medium viability

Each real-medium dataset (Marine, and each freshwater variant separately) is
assessed for sufficiency: it is **viable** if it is backed by a curated source, or
if its uncurated data meet at least 5 species from at least 4 distinct taxonomic
classes (the `class`-based threshold described in the
[Deviations appendix](#sec-deviations)). Curated datasets bypass the count and are
always viable. Of 6,431 real-medium combinations assessed, 1,144 were viable (44
curated-backed, 1,100 on the uncurated threshold) and 5,287 were not.

Each viable real-medium dataset is emitted as its own set, under its own medium
label, with the freshwater variants kept separate throughout.

### The pooled "mixed" set

Toxicity records collected without a stated medium ("Unknown") are never presented
as a standalone set, because an SSD is medium-specific. Instead, for each chemical,
a pooled `mixed` set is assembled from everything that did not form a viable
standalone set — the records of any non-viable real medium, together with the
Unknown records. The pool is emitted only if it itself meets the ≥5 species / ≥4
classes threshold.

Two rules keep the pool meaningful. First, records belonging to a *viable*
standalone medium are not added to the pool, and any species already represented in
a standalone set for that chemical is removed from its mixed set — so a chemical's
`mixed` set never duplicates its single-medium sets. Second, if a chemical has a
viable Freshwater *and* a viable Marine set, its Unknown records are discarded
rather than pooled: with both real media already represented, a medium-agnostic
set adds little. Here 9,221 Unknown records were dropped on this rule, and 438
mixed sets were emitted.

A `mixed` set pools data across media and is therefore not a single-medium SSD; it
exists as a usable fallback for chemicals that no single medium can support. Its
set name carries the `_mixed` suffix, and the per-record `Medium` column inside the
set still records each record's original medium.

### The data object

`allchronic_data` is a single tibble with one row per species × chemical × medium
and a `Set` key column of the form `{chemical}_{medium}` (e.g. `copper_marine`,
`atrazine_mixed`). `ssd_data_sets(set = "all_chronic")` returns this split by
`Set` into a **named list**, each element a ready-to-fit dataset. Column names
follow the package PascalCase convention and are documented at `?allchronic_data`;
`ValueTier` records how each value was derived (`accepted`, `chronic_converted`,
`acute_acr`, or `curated`); `EffectCategory` records the biological effect type of
the selected value (NA for curated rows).

The object contains 26,533 rows (24 columns) across 1,582 sets, covering 1,180
chemicals and 2,801 species: 887 freshwater sets, 254 marine, 438 mixed, and one
each of soft, hard, and moderate freshwater. By derivation, 7,074 rows are accepted
negligible-effect values, 2,764 converted chronic values, 17,091 ACR-converted
acute values, and 770 curated values. The build is reproducible by running
`data-raw/alldata/DATASET.R` from the repository root; the audit report is
`data-raw/alldata/stage7-eligibility-report.md`.

---

## References {#sec-references}

<div id="refs"></div>

---

## Appendix A — Decisions table {#sec-decisions}

One row per named decision confirmed during pipeline planning. Decisions that
were reconsidered or reversed are noted; the most recent decision for each ID
is the operative one.

| Decision ID | Stage | Decision taken | Alternatives considered | Rationale | ANZG reference |
|-------------|-------|---------------|------------------------|-----------|----------------|
| I2 | 4c | Cross-source deduplication applied before ANZG priority selection | Apply priority selection first | Prevents acute data from a high-priority source displacing chronic data from a lower-priority source | — |
| G2 | 4c | Duplicate rows flagged and retained (not dropped) | Drop duplicates immediately | Preserves full audit trail; retained flag allows inspection | — |
| H2 | 4c | `native_cas` used as dedup key (not `casnumber_grouped`) | Use `casnumber_grouped` | At dedup time, parent grouping had not yet been applied | — |
| J1 | 4c | 0.1% concentration tolerance for cross-source duplicate matching | Exact match; higher tolerance | Accounts for floating-point representation differences without over-merging | — |
| J2b | 4c | NA in dedup key fields treated as non-matching (strict NA) | Treat NA as wildcard | Conservative; avoids false duplicate assignments | — |
| J3a | 4c | Medium treated strictly in dedup key | Ignore medium in dedup | Records from different media are not duplicates of each other | — |
| P3 | 4d | Single-source taxonomy pooling with wqbench > envirotox > anztox tiebreaker; fields not mixed across sources | Mix fields across sources | Avoids inconsistencies from combining taxonomy from different ontologies | — |
| R1 | 4d → 4e | **Reversed 2026-06-26.** "Genus sp." / genus-rank entries in uncurated sources are EXCLUDED (not counted as species); curated sources unaffected | Original R1 retained them at genus level | A genus-level identification may represent several species, so it cannot stand in for one species in an SSD; excluding is the conservative, defensible choice | §3.4.4 |
| U3 | 4d | Source-native taxonomy used as fallback hierarchy for problem species; `accepted_name` never fabricated | Fabricate accepted_name from genus; drop problem species | Preserves data while maintaining honesty about resolution quality | — |
| D1 | 4e | NA `life_stage` treated as a distinct group level (not equivalent to any named value) | Drop NA life_stage rows; collapse NA with named values | Conservative; unknown life stage should not be assumed equivalent to a known one | §3.4.4 |
| D2 | 4e | Geometric mean replaced by minimum where within-group spread > 1 order of magnitude | Flag only; keep geometric mean; require manual review | Automated, reproducible, conservative; preserves audit trail via `geomean_flagged` | §3.4.4 |
| D3 | 4e | Eligibility check (≥5 species / ≥4 groups) deferred to Stage 7 | Apply in Stage 4e | Stage 4e output is a complete reference file; threshold is a single-point-of-control in Stage 7 | §3.5 |
| D4 | 4e | Per-row dedup audit columns dropped; provenance summarised as `n_records`, `sources_contributing`, `any_acr_applied`, `geomean_flagged` | Retain all input columns | Per-row dedup columns are not meaningful at the aggregated level | — |
| D5 | 4e | NA `duration_hours` treated as a distinct group level (199 rows retained, not dropped) | Drop NA duration rows | Consistent with NA life_stage treatment; records are legitimate and 199 rows is non-trivial | §3.4.4 |
| D6 | 4e (patch) | Concentration plausibility filter applied before aggregation: hard exclude < 10⁻⁵ and > 10⁸ µg/L; soft flag 10⁻⁵–10⁻³ and 10⁶–10⁸ µg/L; geomean computed from ok-range records only, with soft-only fallback | No filter; filter only at Stage 6; hard exclude at soft threshold | Addresses physically implausible values (unit errors) at the correct pipeline layer; soft threshold preserves legitimate edge cases with audit trail | — |
| D7 | 4e | Statistic types classified into accepted / convert / exclude; ECx percentile parsed (incl. decimals) | Treat all estimates as negligible-effect; exclude all median/low-effect | Aligns the dataset with the §3.2.4 hierarchy; only estimates with a defined treatment enter the SSD | §3.2.4 |
| D8 | 4e | Chronic/subchronic median- and low-effect estimates converted: EC/IC/LC50 ÷5, LOEC/LOEL ÷2.5, MATC ÷2 | Exclude them entirely; keep raw (silently treated as negligible-effect) | Implements the §3.4.2.1 conversion; preserves species that would otherwise be lost, while flagging the conversion | §3.4.2.1 |
| D9 | 4e | Undefined ECx percentiles (20<x<50, x>50) and regulatory endpoints (NOAEL/LOAEL/NOAEC/LOAEC/MCIG) excluded | Convert with an assumed factor; retain raw | No defined @Warne2025ANZG treatment; small counts; no defensible negligible-effect mapping | §3.2.4 |
| D10 | 4e | Three-tier preference (accepted > chronic-converted > acute-ACR) applied per species × chemical × medium, across all endpoints | Apply per endpoint; rely on upstream chronic>acute dedup; treat all converted data as equal | Realises the §3.2.4 "in order of preference" ranking at admission; ensures converted values are a true fallback and chronic outranks acute | §3.2.4, §3.4.2.2 |
| S6-D1 | 6 | ANZG and CCME used wholesale, no aggregation | Apply @Warne2025ANZG Section 3.4.4 aggregation | Jurisdictionally authoritative; curator-reviewed values should not be modified | — |
| S6-D2 | 6 | AIMS and CSIRO: WoRMS/GBIF taxonomy resolution applied before aggregation | Use source-native taxonomy directly | Required for consistent `accepted_name` and synonym unification across sources | — |
| S6-D3 | 6 | `Domain` field excluded from AIMS/CSIRO aggregation grouping key | Include Domain in grouping key | Consistent with `.apply_dedup()` behaviour; same-species records from different domains averaged | §3.4.4 |
| S6-D4 | 6 | Curated rows with no species name dropped at load (no placeholder taxon); removes CSIRO chlorine/marine acute rows | Coin an "Unknown species"; retain with NA taxonomy | An SSD value must attach to a named taxon; chlorine/marine is acute and out of scope for a chronic dataset | — |
| S6-D5 | 6 | Exclusion at `casnumber_grouped × medium` level (full combination, not species-level) | Species-level overlap check only | Prevents partial exclusion where only species-level overlaps would be matched | — |
| S6-D6 | 6 | ANZG freshwater exclusion uses broad matching across all five freshwater medium variants | Exact medium string matching | All five ANZG freshwater variants treated as equivalent for exclusion purposes | — |
| S6-D7 | 6 | CCME treated as Freshwater (Issue #34 pending confirmation) | Treat as Unknown pending confirmation | Observed medium values in `ccme_data` are `"Freshwater"`; pipeline proceeds on observed data | — |
| S6-D8 | 6 | 18 UNCERTAIN CAS rows excluded via `excluded` column in master lookup | Include UNCERTAIN rows | Unusual chemicals unlikely to meet ≥5 species threshold; common chemicals superseded by curated sources | — |
| S7-D1 | 7 | Sufficiency filter applied to uncurated rows only; curated sources bypass | Apply filter to all rows | Curated sources are pre-approved datasets already confirmed to meet data requirements | §3.4.5 |
| S7-D2 | 7 | Taxonomic groups for sufficiency defined as `class` (resolved WoRMS/GBIF class), counting only non-NA values | Use ANZG Table 5 groups | `class` is directly available, reproducible, and consistent with the wqbench package convention | §3.4.5 |
| S7-D3 | 7 | ANZG freshwater medium variants not collapsed for sufficiency counting or in output | Collapse to "Freshwater" for counting | Variants reflect distinct physicochemical conditions documented by ANZG; collapsing would lose this information | — |
| S7-D4 | 7 | `allchronic_data` object name; `ssd_data_sets(set = "all_chronic")` set name | `alldata_data` / `"alldata"` | Name accurately reflects chronic-only scope; leaves `allacute_data` / `"all_acute"` available for a future pipeline | — |
| S7-D5 | 7 | Stage 6 Phase 2 and Stage 7 logic consolidated into `data-raw/alldata/DATASET.R` inline | Keep scripts separate | Single entry-point for rebuilding the final data object; consistent with the DATASET.R pattern used for other package data | — |
| S4E-D1 | 4e | Stage 4e aggregation consolidated into `data-raw/alldata/DATASET.R` | Keep as a separate script (`data-raw/alldata/scripts/stage4e-aggregate.R`) | Single entry-point for all artefacts that write to `data-raw/alldata/`; Stage 4e output passes in-memory to Stage 6, removing the two-step run requirement | — |
| S7-D6 | 7 | Output is a named list keyed `{chemical}_{medium}`, built from a flat tibble with a `Set` column | Single flat tibble; list keyed by CAS | Matches `ssd_data_sets()`'s per-dataset return shape; chemical-name keys are human-readable | — |
| S7-D7 | 7 | A real-medium set (marine, or each freshwater variant) is emitted only if viable: curated-backed or ≥5 species / ≥4 classes | Apply one threshold per chemical pooling all media | Each emitted set is a usable single-medium SSD in its own right | §3.4.5 |
| S7-D8 | 7 | Unknown records never form a standalone set; pooled into a `mixed` set (with non-viable real-medium records) only when the pool meets ≥5/≥4 | Emit standalone Unknown sets; merge Unknown into a real medium | SSDs are medium-specific; a medium-agnostic set is a labelled fallback, not a single-medium claim | — |
| S7-D9 | 7 | Viable single-medium records excluded from the pool; species already in a standalone set removed from that chemical's mixed set; Unknown dropped entirely when both Freshwater and Marine are viable | Always pool all records; allow overlap | Prevents a mixed set from shadowing or duplicating single-medium sets (the ">50% overlap" failure mode) | — |

---

## Appendix B — Deviations from @Warne2025ANZG {#sec-deviations}

This pipeline produces a dataset for SSD methodology testing, not for official
guideline derivation. Several deliberate deviations from strict ANZG procedure
are documented here. None of these deviations affect the scientific validity of
the dataset for its intended purpose.

| Deviation | Stage | Description | Justification | Consequence for use |
|-----------|-------|-------------|---------------|---------------------|
| `majorgroup` = taxonomic class | 4d | Taxonomic groups for eligibility checks are defined as the resolved WoRMS/GBIF class, not the groups in ANZG Table 5 | Simpler, reproducible, consistent with the `wqbench` package's own `filter(n_distinct(class) >= 4)` convention | Slight differences in which taxa are counted as distinct groups versus strict ANZG Table 5; does not affect SSD fitting |
| WoRMS exact-name-priority pre-filter | 4d | Where WoRMS returns both a species record and a subspecies record for the same query name with identical taxonomy, the exact species match is promoted | WoRMS fuzzy=TRUE was conflating species and subspecies entries; the pre-filter prevents false ambiguity | Minor effect; affects a small number of species with subspecies records |
| GBIF genus-or-finer rank floor | 4d | GBIF matches are accepted only at genus rank or finer | Prevents higher-level matches (e.g. family or order) from being treated as species-level resolutions | A small number of species remain unresolved that a less strict floor would partially resolve |
| Default ACR applied at pipeline scale | 4e | A default ACR of 10 is applied to all eligible acute records; chemical-specific or taxon-specific ACRs are not computed | ACRs are not available at scale across thousands of chemicals; @Warne2025ANZG §3.4.2.2 specifies ACR = 10 as the default | Converted-acute values may be less accurate than values derived from empirical ACRs for specific chemicals |
| Curated source taxonomy not fully resolved via WoRMS | 6 | ANZG and CCME species names are trusted as curator-reviewed; WoRMS/GBIF resolution applied only to AIMS and CSIRO | ANZG and CCME are jurisdictionally authoritative; applying WoRMS resolution could introduce name mismatches or overwrite deliberate curatorial choices | ANZG and CCME rows carry `taxonomy_provenance = "curated_source"` with `Class`, `Kingdom`, and related fields absent; users requiring full taxonomy across all sources should handle NAs for these rows |
| Eligibility threshold ≥5 species / ≥4 groups | 7 | The sufficiency threshold used is ≥5 species from ≥4 taxonomic groups, applied to uncurated rows only | More conservative than the ANZG minimum of ≥6 species; chosen deliberately because five was used historically in the anzg and they represent interesting testing cases. This will also allow a wider range of chemicals in the test dataset | More chemicals pass the threshold than would under strict ANZG rules; curated chemicals are included regardless of threshold |
| Per-species (not per-chemical) fallback | 4e | The §3.4.2.1 "insufficient preferred data" fallback is evaluated per species × chemical × medium, not pooled across all species of a chemical | A one-value-per-species benchmark needs each species resolved on its own best-available evidence; pooling at the chemical level would drop species that have only median/low-effect data even when the chemical overall has ample preferred data | A species with only median/low-effect data is retained (with a converted, flagged value) rather than excluded; recorded in `value_tier` / `any_chronic_conv_applied` |
| Cross-medium `mixed` sets | 7 | A `mixed` set pools toxicity data across Freshwater, Marine, and Unknown media into one SSD | Provides a usable dataset for chemicals that no single medium can support at the ≥5/≥4 threshold; the alternative is no SSD at all for those chemicals | A `_mixed` set is not a single-medium SSD; users should treat it as a medium-agnostic screening set. The per-record `Medium` column inside the set preserves each record's original medium |
